paragraphs.body: Don’t even try to go to sleep at a reasonable time wait until she is in bed; clean up after her messes; let her take your stuff without asking; never borrow something of hers with asking because she will turn your words; don’t make noise at 7am; if she makes noise at 7am and wakes you up it’s ok; Always be ok with her having group facetimes calls with no headphones at 1am on a Tuesday night; Be careful not to have a facetime call in the room at 3pm; Remember to let your roommate have people over to stay the night and sleep in your bed without asking; Don’t turn all the lights off if she is still up, make sure to keep the soft lighting on – the lamp and string lights you bought; When she turns the all the lights off while your still up let it happen; Don’t speak up to her if what she does bother you; But I have tried to speak up to her; If you pay for the both of you with agreement of her to venmo you what she owes NEVER expect to receive the venmo no matter how much you ask for it; never expect her to pay for anything; Let her skip your shared class and never show up, but you have to always go so she never misses material; Now if you are sick or have an obligation that prevents you from going to the shared class don’t expect her to go and get the information from another classmate; If she leaves her dirty dishes by the bathroom sink don’t expect her to clean her it, instead it will sit there and rot for days until you get so fed up you do it; If she orders doordash on a Saturday expect to see the food still sitting there on desk until the following Saturday; Don’t leave your half-eaten leftovers in the fridge until they grow mold; don’t let your coffee cups pile up on your desk until they form their own civilization; don’t leave your shoes in the middle of the floor so I trip over them in the dark; don’t forget to take out the trash until the entire room smells like a landfill; Stay quiet or else your life will be a living hell; I want to speak up; Be careful of what you tell her because she will use it against you; Be strong and don’t respond or show a reaction to her passive aggressive remarks she makes all the time; Privacy doesn’t exist anymore even when you ask; Don’t have a telehealth call in your room when you knew she would have class at that time, but she always skips so go into the dorm lounge with everyone else; If you want two hours alone in the room don’t expect it even is she has class because she won’t go; If possible go home every weekend to find peace of mind; If you have people over after asking if it is ok be ready for the most uncomfortable mood ever to the point you just go to your friends dorm; If you are asleep be prepared to wake up to the weighted dorm door slamming close; If you want to take a nap you will have to sleep only when she is sleeping because she won’t care if she is up; Remember calling your mom will be your best friends so you don’t blow up on her and then you are suddenly the bad guy; Going to random cafes after classes and not coming back until late will be the best way for alone time; Don’t treat our dorm like your personal hotel where I’m the unpaid maid; don’t expect me to be okay with your mess, your noise, your laziness, your excuses; Don’t act shocked when I stop cleaning up after you; Don’t call me uptight when I ask for basic respect; Don’t make me regret ever agreeing to share a room with you; Don’t forget that this is my space too; You were never a confrontational person, so don’t start now; I need to start now.

annotations.text: treat our dorm like your personal hotel where I’m the unpaid maid; don’t expect me to be okay with your mess, your noise, your laziness, your excuses; Don’t act shocked when I stop cleaning up after you; Don’t call me uptight when I ask for basic respect; Don’t make me regret ever agreeing to share a room with you; Don’t forget that this is my space too;
annotations.comment: Again, I'm not sure why you switched to the first person occasionally.

annotations.text: Don’t even try to go to sleep at a reasonable time wait until she is in bed; clean up after her messes; let her take your stuff without asking; never borrow something of hers with asking because she will turn your words; don’t make noise at 7am; if she makes noise at 7am and wakes you up it’s ok
annotations.comment: You do a great job of setting the tone and center of conflict for this piece immediately. Your reader will get a sense from the very first lines that this relationship is not reciprocal.
I'm not sure about the first-person here--it's been about her, why is it now about me?

Hello, The central focusing idea of this piece is that it is very difficult to live with another person. The big ideas that you use to support this notion are the rules that others have for themselves, the rules they have for you, and how you attempt to navigate the difference between those two sets of rules. The word that stood out more than anything else to me comes in the first sentence and only occurs once in the entire piece: reasonable. This word really sets the tone of the essay by showing how reasonable you are being and how unreasonable you believe your roommate is. Each example you provide shows, as I mentioned above, how she is allowed to behave one way but insists that you do as she says, rather than as she, herself does. The thing that I admire most about this essay is that while you do mention many of your roommate’s bad behaviors, you also admit to many of your own—this essay is not about blaming your roommate or shaming your roommate, only about the hypocrisies of her rules for appropriate conduct. The audience for this piece at first might seem as though it is your roommate, but I think it is much broader than that. The truth is that anybody who is sharing a room with a stranger for the first time is going to go through this or another very similar experience. Almost everybody on the planet has a set of rules they themselves follow and another set of rules they expect everybody else to just know—we just have different degrees to which we enforce the rules or react to someone else breaking them. And knowing this would be good for any young person about to have this experience. What I’ve learned from this piece is that you are recognizing what it means to live in such close proximity to another person. You know that you need to address your concerns and frustrations, and that, too, is a part of learning how to interact with others. If she doesn’t know how her behavior affects you, she will never have any impetus to change—she might not even know she is bothering you. At any rate, it was a pleasure to read this piece. Thanks,
